description: Real-world math missions from the document's activity framework. Kids do these at home or outside — no screen needed except to read the mission!

missions.title: Number Hunt
missions.description: Go on a number hunt! See how many numbers you can find.
steps: Look for numbers on number plates, shop signs, house doors, buses
Call out each number you see as loudly as you can!
For older kids: add up the digits on a number plate
Challenge: find the numbers 1 to 20 in order before you reach your destination!
missions.buddy_prompt: What is the biggest number you found? What is the smallest? Can you count how many number plates you spotted?
missions.parent_tip: Make it competitive — first to spot a number plate with a 7 wins!

missions.title: Kitchen Counting
missions.description: Help in the kitchen and learn counting and measurement at the same time!
steps: Ask your parent what they are cooking today
Count how many spoons of dal go into the pressure cooker
Count how many cups of rice or flour are needed
Pour each one yourself, counting out loud as you go
missions.buddy_prompt: How many spoons did you put in? If we needed double the amount, how many would that be?
missions.parent_tip: Let the child actually measure and pour. The physical action builds number sense far better than worksheets.

missions.title: Smart Scoops
what_you_need: any small objects (marbles, coins, laddoos, stones)
missions.description: Use 'scoops' to visualise addition — the tastiest maths you'll ever do!
steps: Put out 3 bowls. Each bowl is an 'ice cream order'
Order 1: put 2 scoops (objects) in bowl 1
Order 2: put 3 scoops in bowl 2
Put all scoops together: how many total?
Make your own orders and add them up!
missions.buddy_prompt: If I order 4 scoops and you order 5 scoops, how many scoops is that altogether?
missions.parent_tip: Use actual ice cream for a special treat version. Real food makes maths memorable!

missions.title: Human Clock
missions.where: Any open space (living room, terrace, garden)
missions.description: Turn your whole body into a clock and never forget how to tell time!
steps: Draw a giant clock circle on the floor with chalk, OR place a paper plate in the centre
Write 12 at the top, 6 at the bottom, 3 on the right, 9 on the left
Stand in the middle. Your RIGHT arm is the minute hand, LEFT arm is the hour hand
Parent calls out a time: '3 o'clock!' — child points both arms correctly
Take turns calling times and showing them with your body!
missions.buddy_prompt: What time do you wake up for school? Can you show me that time? What time do you eat lunch?
missions.concept: Telling time, clock reading, spatial reasoning
missions.parent_tip: Start with o'clock times, then half past, then quarter past/to. Build up gradually.

missions.title: Target Toss
what_you_need: any small object to toss (eraser, stone, coin)
missions.description: Build a target on the floor and make 3-digit numbers by tossing!
steps: Draw three large rings on paper: inner ring = HUNDREDS, middle = TENS, outer = ONES
Toss your eraser from 1 metre away
Where it lands tells you the place value: inner=5 hundreds, middle=3 tens, outer=7 ones
Write down your number (537 in this example)
Both players toss 3 times each. Whoever makes the BIGGEST number wins!
missions.buddy_prompt: If you got 4 in the hundreds ring, 2 in the tens ring, and 8 in the ones ring — what number did you make?
missions.parent_tip: After a few rounds, add: whose number is closest to 500? This introduces approximation.

missions.title: Salute! (The Card Game)
missions.where: Anywhere with a deck of cards
missions.description: A brilliant two-player game that builds mental arithmetic speed!
steps: Player A and Player B each hold ONE card facing OUTWARD on their forehead (don't look!)
A third person (parent/sibling) looks at BOTH cards and says the SUM
Example: cards are 7 and 4. Leader says '11!'
Each player must figure out THEIR OWN card by looking at the other person's
Player A sees 4 on B's head, hears 11: 'My card must be 7!' — First correct answer wins the round!
missions.buddy_prompt: The leader said 15. You can see the other person's card is 8. What is YOUR card?
missions.concept: Mental subtraction, algebraic thinking (unknown + known = total)
missions.parent_tip: Once mastered with addition, use MULTIPLICATION as the rule instead. Harder but brilliant!

missions.title: Maths Scavenger Hunt
missions.description: Find real objects that match given measurements — maths detective at home!
steps: Write a mission list: Find something that is 20cm long. Find something that weighs about 200g. Find something roughly 1 litre. Find something that fits in your palm.
Give the child a ruler and scale
They hunt around the house, measure, and write down what they find
Compare results — did you find the same things?
missions.buddy_prompt: What was the heaviest thing you found? What was the longest? Did anything surprise you?
missions.concept: Measurement (length, weight, volume), estimation, real-world application
missions.parent_tip: Make it competitive with siblings. Give a time limit of 20 minutes. The one who finds items closest to the target measurements wins.

missions.title: Grocery Budget Run
missions.description: Give the child a shopping budget and a list — they manage the money!
steps: Before going: write a list of 5-6 items you actually need to buy
Give the child ₹200 (or show them the budget)
Their job: estimate the cost of each item BEFORE checking the price
At the shop: check actual prices, add them up, decide what fits in the budget
At checkout: let them count the money and check the change
missions.buddy_prompt: Which items were more expensive than you expected? Did you have to make any hard choices about what not to buy? How much change did you get?
missions.concept: Addition, estimation, budgeting, decimals (prices), real-world decision making
missions.parent_tip: If anything is left over, it's theirs to keep! This motivation is extraordinary for engagement.

missions.title: Count Down Challenge
missions.description: Pick a 3-digit target. Use 6 random numbers and the 4 operations to reach it — like the TV game show!
steps: Generate a target: roll a die 3 times to make a 3-digit number (e.g., 347)
Shuffle number chits (1-9) and pick 6 at random
Using ONLY those 6 numbers and +, -, ×, ÷, try to reach 347
You don't have to use all 6 numbers
Closest answer in 3 minutes wins!
missions.buddy_prompt: What was your target? How close did you get? What operations did you use?
missions.concept: All four operations, order of operations, strategic thinking
missions.parent_tip: This is directly from the British game show 'Countdown'. Look it up on YouTube with your child for inspiration!

missions.title: Kitchen Fractions
what_you_need: Any food that can be divided: chapati, pizza, cake, watermelon
missions.description: Use real food to understand fractions — the most delicious maths lesson ever!
steps: Take one chapati or piece of bread. This is your WHOLE (1/1)
Cut it in HALF: each piece is 1/2. How many halves make a whole?
Cut into QUARTERS: each piece is 1/4. How many quarters make one half?
Now the challenge questions: If we eat 3 quarters, what fraction is left? (1/4)
If the chapati had 8 parts and we eat 3, what fraction is left? (5/8)
missions.buddy_prompt: We cut the pizza into 8 slices. Your family ate 5 slices. What fraction is left? What fraction did you eat?
missions.concept: Fractions, equivalent fractions, fraction of a whole
missions.parent_tip: Never let the child see the answer before they try. The struggle to figure it out is what builds the understanding.

missions.title: Recipe Ratio Challenge
what_you_need: A simple recipe (dal, salad dressing, lassi)
missions.description: Double or halve a recipe — and discover ratios in the process!
steps: Pick a simple recipe. Lassi: 2 cups curd, 1 cup water, sugar to taste
Task 1: Double the recipe for more guests. (4 cups curd, 2 cups water)
Task 2: Halve it for fewer people. (1 cup curd, ½ cup water)
Task 3: Make it for exactly 5 people if the recipe serves 2
Discuss: what stayed the same? The RATIO of curd to water (2:1) never changed!
missions.buddy_prompt: The recipe uses 3 cups of oil and 1 cup of vinegar. What is the ratio? If you triple the recipe, how much oil and vinegar do you need?
missions.parent_tip: Actually cook the recipe together after the maths. Children who cook with parents develop lifelong quantitative thinking.

missions.title: Room Designer Challenge
missions.description: Measure a real room, design it on graph paper, then calculate area and perimeter!
steps: Choose a room: bedroom, hall, or living room
Measure its length and width using a measuring tape
Draw it on graph paper: 1 square = 1 foot (or 10cm)
Calculate the AREA (how much floor space?) and PERIMETER (how much wall?)
Now design the IDEAL room! Add furniture cutouts drawn to scale. Would the sofa fit?
missions.buddy_prompt: Your room is 12 feet long and 10 feet wide. What is its area? If you want to put carpet, and carpet costs ₹50 per square foot, how much would it cost?
missions.concept: Area, perimeter, scale drawing, measurement, multiplication
missions.parent_tip: This activity takes 40-60 minutes but is one of the most powerful geometry learning experiences possible. Many engineers and architects trace their interest to exactly this kind of activity as children.
